_meta.description: PMI-aligned PM ontology. Original work grounded in public PMI framework documentation. Does not reproduce licensed PMI content.
references: PMI Process Groups: A Practice Guide
PMI Standard for Program Management 5th Ed

Initiating.name: Initiating
Initiating.description: Processes that authorize and define a new project or phase, establishing scope and objectives
Initiating.purpose: Obtain authorization, align stakeholder expectations, define initial scope and constraints

Planning.name: Planning
Planning.description: Processes that develop actionable strategies for achieving project objectives within constraints
Planning.purpose: Define scope, schedule, budget, quality, resource, communications, risk, and procurement approaches

Executing.name: Executing
Executing.description: Processes that complete the work defined in the project management plan to satisfy project specifications
Executing.purpose: Produce deliverables, manage team performance, execute procurements, implement quality assurance

Monitoring & Controlling.name: Monitoring & Controlling
Monitoring & Controlling.description: Processes that track, review, and regulate project progress and performance; identify areas requiring changes
Monitoring & Controlling.purpose: Measure performance against baselines, manage changes, verify deliverables, monitor risks

Closing.name: Closing
Closing.description: Processes that finalize all activities to formally complete or close the project, phase, or contract
Closing.purpose: Transfer deliverables, archive records, release resources, document lessons learned, obtain final acceptance

Integration Management.description: Unifies and coordinates project management processes and activities across all knowledge areas

Scope Management.description: Ensures the project includes all and only the work required to complete it successfully

Schedule Management.description: Ensures timely completion of the project through sequencing, estimation, and schedule control

Cost Management.description: Ensures the project is completed within the approved budget through estimation, budgeting, and cost control

keyFormulas.CPI: EV ÷ AC  (>1.0 = under budget; <1.0 = over budget)
keyFormulas.TCPI: (BAC − EV) ÷ (BAC − AC)  (efficiency needed to finish on budget)

Quality Management.description: Ensures the project and deliverables satisfy the requirements for which they were undertaken

Resource Management.description: Identifies, acquires, and manages the resources needed to successfully execute the project

Communications Management.description: Ensures timely, appropriate planning, collection, creation, distribution, and management of project information

Risk Management.description: Increases likelihood and impact of positive events while decreasing likelihood and impact of negative events

Procurement Management.description: Processes to purchase or acquire products, services, or results needed from outside the project team

contractTypes.Fixed Price: FFP, FPIF, FP-EPA — seller bears cost risk
contractTypes.Cost Reimbursable: CPFF, CPIF, CPAF — buyer bears cost risk
contractTypes.Time & Materials: T&M — hybrid; suitable for ambiguous scope

Stakeholder Management.description: Identifies and manages the people, groups, and organizations that can affect or be affected by the project

Technical.description: Risks arising from technology, tools, technical feasibility, system integration, or architecture complexity

Organizational.description: Risks from staffing, skills, culture, organizational change, or resource availability
examples: Change fatigue from multiple simultaneous initiatives

External.description: Risks from forces outside the project team's control: regulatory, market, environmental, geopolitical

Schedule.description: Risks that directly threaten timeline and milestone achievement

Financial.description: Risks affecting project budget, funding continuity, or financial performance

Stakeholder.description: Risks from stakeholder behavior, resistance, competing priorities, or disengagement

Unaware.definition: Stakeholder is unaware of the project and its potential impact
Unaware.riskIfUnaddressed: Stakeholder blindsided at critical moment; reactive resistance

Resistant.definition: Stakeholder is aware of the project but opposes or resists the change it represents
characteristics: Often has legitimate concerns worth understanding
strategies: Demonstrate WIIFM (What's In It For Me)
Leverage respected peers as change agents
Resistant.riskIfUnaddressed: Active sabotage, escalations to leadership, organizational friction

Neutral.definition: Stakeholder is aware of the project but neither supports nor opposes it
characteristics: Fence-sitter; easily moved in either direction
Often has capacity to become Supportive with right engagement
strategies: Regular updates highlighting relevance to their priorities
Involve in decisions affecting their domain
Neutral.riskIfUnaddressed: Drift toward resistance; missed opportunity for advocacy

Supportive.definition: Stakeholder is aware of and supports the project and its objectives
characteristics: Will provide information and resources when asked
strategies: Invite to be spokesperson with peers
Supportive.riskIfUnaddressed: Support erodes if they feel taken for granted or uninformed

Leading.definition: Stakeholder actively champions the project and leads others toward its success
strategies: Empower with decision authority in their domain
Leading.riskIfUnaddressed: Champion burnout; loss of most effective change agent

variables.BAC: Budget at Completion — total approved project budget
variables.PV: Planned Value — budgeted cost of work scheduled through reporting date
variables.EV: Earned Value — budgeted cost of work actually completed through reporting date
variables.AC: Actual Cost — total costs incurred for work completed through reporting date

EAC_typical.use: When current performance is expected to continue

EAC_atypical.use: When current variance is a one-time event

EAC_with_SPI.use: When both cost and schedule are concerns

TCPI.interpretation: Required CPI to complete on budget; >1.0 means must work more efficiently than to date

programManagement.definitionVsProject: A program is a group of related projects managed in a coordinated way to obtain benefits not available from managing them individually.

benefitsRealization.definition: The practice of ensuring project/program outcomes are translated into measurable business benefits

governanceStructure.programBoard: Decision-making body providing strategic direction and resolving escalated issues
governanceStructure.programManager: Accountable for benefit delivery and stakeholder alignment across constituent projects
governanceStructure.projectManagers: Accountable for individual project delivery within program constraints
governanceStructure.PMO: Provides standards, methods, and oversight across the program portfolio

projectCharter.purpose: Formally authorize the project and provide authority to the project manager

riskRegister.purpose: Central repository for all identified risks, their analyses, and response plans

raciMatrix.purpose: Clarify roles and responsibilities for all major deliverables and work packages

definitions.A: Accountable — owns the outcome; approves completion; only one per row

rules: Exactly one A per row (work package)
At least one R per row

stakeholderEngagementPlan.purpose: Define strategies to move stakeholders from current to desired engagement level

statusReport.purpose: Communicate project health, progress, and forecast to stakeholders

ragDefinitions.Green: On track; no issues requiring escalation
ragDefinitions.Amber: Concerns exist but manageable within current plan; monitoring required
ragDefinitions.Red: Significant issue; requires immediate attention or decision from leadership

lessonsLearned.purpose: Capture knowledge from the project to improve future performance
lessonsLearned.processGroup: Closing (also during execution for long projects)
bestPractices: Conduct during project, not only at end
Focus on systemic issues not individuals
Action the recommendations — don't shelf the document
